informal: Prove that there are unique positive integers $a, n$ such that $a^{n+1}-(a+1)^n=2001$.
reasoning: The Lean signature encodes the pair (a, n) as p : ℕ × ℕ with p.1, p.2 > 0, exactly matching "positive integers a, n". The equation a^(n+1) - (a+1)^n = 2001 uses ℕ truncated subtraction, but since the required value is the positive number 2001, any solution in ℕ must satisfy a^(n+1) ≥ (a+1)^n + 2001, in which case the truncated subtraction agrees with integer subtraction; conversely any integer solution gives a ℕ solution. So the solution sets coincide. The use of ∃! correctly captures "unique" existence of such a pair. No extra hypotheses are added and none are dropped.
